title: Discrimination in the payments chain
abstract: We examine whether discrimination affects customers’ willingness to pay their suppliers. Using a dataset of detailed trade credit networks, we find that when facing a macroeconomic shock, customers delay payments to their suppliers with female or black trade credit officers at a 10%–20% higher rate relative to their payments to non-minorities. These results hold after controlling for a host of economic differences between minority groups and non-minority groups. In particular, we exploit the complexity of the supply chain network – wherein suppliers transact with multiple customers in each month and customers transact with multiple suppliers in each month – to estimate within-relationship changes in payment behavior during periods of financial hardship. Results indicate that the largest increases in payment delays are between customers that are classified as having racial or gender biases and suppliers that have minority lead credit officers. The results suggest that biased beliefs and preferences play a critical role in trade credit.

author.Anna M. Costello*: University of Chicago, Booth School of Business, United States of America
author.Michael Minnis: University of Chicago, Booth School of Business, United States of America

article.1. Introduction: Understanding the role of discrimination in the marketplace is of critical importance from both social and economic perspectives. There is significant disparity in hiring, compensation, and promotion across minority versus non-minority groups. For example, in the U.S., women earn 77 cents for every dollar earned by men; the divide is similar along racial minorities, with blacks earning 76 cents for every dollar earned by whites.1 These disparities have been shown to hinder economic growth (Hsieh et al., 2019), and thus there is a growing interest in uncovering the potential role that discrimination plays in differential outcomes for minority groups. Understanding the scope and types of discrimination can help guide interventions aimed at closing performance gaps.We seek to identify the role of discrimination in supply chain relationships. Discrimination can impact multiple aspects of the supply chain including partner selection (choosing which suppliers or customers to transact with), pricing strategies, and other contractual features. We focus on the potential role of discrimination in the payment behavior of customers. Trade credit – wherein a supplier provides temporary financing to a customer purchasing a product or service – is common practice in business-to-business transactions. Up to 90% of inter-firm trade is supported by trade finance (Auboin, 2009), and prior work shows that trade credit helps to ease a multitude of transactional frictions (e.g., Giannetti et al., 2011, Costello, 2019 and Love et al. (2007)). Suppliers rely on the timely collection of their receivables to meet working capital demands, execute planned expenditures on labor and capital, and manage other cash flow needs. However, there may be variation in a supplier’s ability to collect their accounts receivable. We ask whether demographic characteristics of the supplier’s trade credit officer – specifically whether they are female or Black – impacts their collection of payments due from customers.An important aspect of our setting is that the suppliers and customers are in long-established relationships; the average relationship “age” in our sample is 12.5 years. One view may be that long-run relationships should filter out bias, since parties have already chosen to select into the relationship, they have a long track record of observing their partner’s performance, and their transactions are more relational than arms-length.2 Therefore, it is an open question whether discriminatory preferences can persist even after a relationship is long-established.A priori, it is not clear that discrimination manifests in differential economic outcomes for minority groups versus non-minority groups. Market forces should serve a disciplining role in mitigating discriminatory actions. For example, Becker (1957) and Arrow (1973) point out that discrimination should not impact wages under conditions of perfect competition, since a less-prejudiced employer can earn higher profits by hiring cheaper minority laborers. Similarly, suppliers in competitive markets may punish non-paying customers by rationing supply. Thus, we expect that discrimination should play a more prominent role in customers’ payment choices in settings where market-based punishments for non-payment are weakened.As a setting to capture discrimination, we rely on the immediate onset of COVID-19 in March of 2020. The pandemic is an appealing setting to empirically capture the effects of discrimination for two primary reasons. First, it represents a large, economy-wide shock in customers’ ability to pay their bills. The onset of the crisis marked unprecedented losses in the corporate sector, when mandatory stay-at-home orders necessitated that many businesses temporarily shut down. This large economic shock likely forced customers to make decisions on which invoices to pay, and which suppliers landed lower in the pecking order. Second, suppliers faced widespread uncertainty about customer demand and their ability to sell products downstream, leading to a reduction in suppliers’ ability to punish non-paying customers by, e.g., dropping those customers or rationing supply. Thus, the confluence of customers having to make strategic liquidity decisions in addition to weakened suppliers’ ability to punish those decisions likely facilitated a substantive increase in discriminatory behavior, relative to the pre-COVID period.To capture decisions in the payments chain, we rely on a proprietary database of detailed trade transactions between buyers and sellers. The data not only provides the identity of buyer–supplier company pairs, but it also provides information on the current and overdue accounts receivable. For each buyer–supplier pair, the information is updated on a monthly basis, which allows us to track customer payment behavior in a tight window around the onset of the pandemic.The data includes the identity of the primary contact that the customer paying the bill will interact with: the supplier’s lead credit officer. The credit officer screens customers at the onset of the relationship to approve trade credit loans, monitors the customer throughout the relationship to make adjustments to the customer’s credit line, and is responsible for collecting payment on each invoice. The trade credit officer and the paying customer often know each other and correspond frequently on the phone or via email. Thus, the supplier’s lead credit officer is the customer’s main point of contact when making payments on outstanding invoices. If discrimination plays a role in the customer’s payment decision, the supplier’s lead credit officer is likely to be the target. We code the names of the lead credit officer by matching them against U.S. census data, which allows us to classify them along two important dimensions: (1) gender, and (2) race, whereby we classify female and black officers as minorities.3Fig. 1 provides visual support for the differential trends in payment behavior. Note the substantial increase in delayed payments to minority suppliers, relative to non-minority suppliers at the onset of COVID. We rely on a differences-in-differences estimation to measure differential changes in overdue accounts for suppliers with minority credit officers after the onset of COVID. In our baseline analyses, we find that suppliers with minority lead credit officers suffer a 10 to 20% larger increase in overdue accounts, relative to suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers.As with all studies on discrimination, the key challenge is to rule out differences in fundamentals between firms that employ minority lead credit officers (“minority suppliers”) versus those that employ non-minority lead credit officers (“non-minority suppliers”). For example, one may be concerned that minority suppliers may be more financially constrained or may be concentrated in certain industries, such as retail or services, and it is these economic differences that result in differential payments, rather than customers’ discriminatory preferences per se.The granularity of the transactions in our data provides several key advantages in addressing this concern. Buyers and sellers in the data transact frequently — often with repeated transactions each month. Further, in a given month, each supplier in the dataset transacts with multiple customers while, concurrently in that same month, each customer purchases from multiple suppliers. The resulting dataset is a rich, time-series of trade credit transactions for a complex buyer-seller network. This allows for rigorous empirical specifications that allow us to simultaneously absorb time-varying supplier characteristics (since each supplier transacts with multiple customers in a month), time-varying customer characteristics (since each customer transacts with multiple suppliers in a month), and time invariant relationship characteristics (since each buyer and seller pair transact in multiple months). The remaining variation that we are able to exploit is the time-varying payment behavior within a given buyer–supplier relationship (a specification we refer to as the within-relationship estimator). This allows us to explore how trade credit payments change at the onset of COVID, within the supplier-customer relationship. Note that our within-relationship estimator allows us to abstract away from many alternative, economics-based, explanations for variation in trade credit payments during COVID. For example, it helps to rule out concerns that minority suppliers may be concentrated in industries that faced supply constraints and/or demand shrinkage; minority suppliers may have been disproportionally impacted by changes in childcare during COVID, making it more difficult for them to exert collection effort; and/or minority suppliers are disproportionally “matched” with certain customer types that more harshly responded to COVID.Download: Download high-res image (240KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Payment delays through COVID-19. This figure displays the average monthly dollar amount of credit that is overdue to a supplier from a single customer, split by whether the lead credit manager for the seller is a Minority or a non-Minority. Minority suppliers are classified as either female or Black.Our within-relationship estimator allows us to target differential payment behavior in the relationships we expect to face the highest degree of discrimination: namely, when “biased” customers are paired with minority suppliers. In other words, controlling for much of the economic factors that drive payment behavior, our within-relationship analysis helps to more convincingly speak to the potential effects of discrimination in the relationships where it should play the strongest role.4 To identify these relationships, we need an empirical construct that captures customers’ biased preferences. To capture customers that have a higher propensity to discriminate, we use five proxies of discriminatory preferences that are measured at the customer’s location-level: (1) whether they are located in a county with high rates of racial terror lynchings during the period 1877–1950; (2) whether they are located in a city classified as a “Sundown Town” – a city that has historical laws or ordinances designed to keep Black individuals out of the community; (3) whether they have “universalist” attitudes as measured by surveys capturing their preference for care and fairness to others; (4) whether they show implicit biases against women as measured by the Implicit Attitude Test; and (5) whether they are located in a state with a high female wage gap. To capture customer bias in a parsimonious fashion, we conduct principal component analyses to obtain a measure of general biased preferences, a measure of biases on the basis of gender, and a measure of biases on the basis of race. Consistent with our hypothesis, we find the largest increases in delayed payments occur in the relationships between biased customers and suppliers with minority lead credit officers. Our within-relationship estimator brings us closer to ruling out alternative stories, in that the only remaining source of variation in our regression model is time-varying payment behavior between a specific customer–supplier pair. However, we recognize that this specification is not bullet-proof. For example, some supplier-customer relationships may be less economically important than others; those less important relationships may see larger increases in payment delays at the onset of COVID simply because the customer did not prioritize supply from that particular seller when the pandemic hit. If, in fact, the least important bilateral relationships are those between biased customers and suppliers with minority lead credit officers, our inferences may be threatened. As a way to account for this, we control for various measures of bilateral relationship importance, including the number of years a particular buyer and seller have been transacting, the pre-COVID dollar volume of sales between pair, and the distance between the buyer and seller. Indeed, we show that the least important relationships face larger payment delays at the onset of COVID. However, we find that even after controlling for various measures of relationship-level economic importance, our inferences remain. That is, we continue to find that the largest increases in payment delays flow from biased customers to minority suppliers.Finally, to bolster our understanding of the potential role of punishment, we explore the consequences to customers of making late payments to their suppliers. Specifically, the prior literature suggests that discrimination should be “punished” in well-functioning markets; in equilibrium, it is this threat of market-based punishment that keeps discriminatory behavior in check (Becker, 1957, Arrow, 1973). By contrast, when more substantive market-based frictions arise, the ability of the discriminatee to punish the discriminator is weakened, which is why we may see an increase in discriminatory behavior. To explore the role of punishment in our setting, we examine relationship terminations (customer “firing”) after defaults. We show that in the pre-COVID period, defaulting customers are much more likely to get fired in the six month period following default, relative to customers that are timely in their payments. By contrast, during the COVID period we find that defaulting customers are no more likely to get fired by their suppliers. The evidence illustrates that market conditions – through their impact on discriminatees’ ability to punish – impact discriminatory behavior.Our study contributes to the growing literature on discrimination. Our contribution to this literature is threefold. First, our setting allows for within-relationship estimation, which controls for much of the confounding factors that influence minority outcomes; thus, while we cannot prove that our results are a manifestation of discrimination beyond doubt, we get closer to ruling out alternative explanations based on economic fundamentals. Second, we explore discrimination in long-established relationships, whereas much of the prior literature studies the role of discrimination in a selection decision when there is high information asymmetry about a candidate’s type (e.g., Bertrand and Mullainathan (2004) and Fisman et al. (2017)).5 Showing that discriminatory behavior impacts trade credit payments well-after the selection stage suggests that preferences can persist and have longer-term effects. Third, we explore discrimination in a new and economically significant setting – supply chain relationships. Identifying discriminatory behavior in various settings helps guide potential interventions aimed at addressing inequities.We also contribute to the growing literature which aims to understand how customer defaults impact payment chains. Understanding discrimination in the trade credit setting is important, because trade credit plays a substantial and well documented role in financing inter-firm trade. The Bank for International Settlements (2014) estimates that two thirds of global trade is supported by trade credit, and many firms rely on trade credit as an important source of financing (Demirguc-Kunt and Maksimovic, 2001). Given the economic significance of this source of lending, it is important to understand all of the risk factors contributing to the supplier’s probability of collection. Further, understanding factors that contribute to collection is important because credit risk can propagate through networks of linked firms (Kiyotaki and Moore, 1997, Gabaix, 2011, Atalay et al., 2011, Costello, 2020). Prior work tends to focus on why suppliers lend (e.g., Giannetti et al., 2021, Fabbri and Menichini, 2010 and Costello (2019)), but few studies explore the determinants of customers willingness to pay. Further, to this point, the literature has not explored the role of discrimination during adverse credit events. Our study highlights an important determinant of the supplier’s ability to collect: the demographic characteristics of their lead credit officer.The paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 describes our conceptual framework and predictions, Section 3 provides a description of our data, and Section 4 provides a framework for our empirical analyses. Section 5 discusses our main results and Section 6 explores within-relationship variation. In Section 7 we explore punishment for delinquent customers, and in Section 8 we discuss several robustness analyses. Finally, Section 9 concludes.
article.2. Conceptual framework and setting: As outlined in the introduction, we argue that COVID is a good setting to test for increases in discriminatory behavior for two primary reasons. The first is that COVID represents a large economic shock that pushes customers into considerations of strategic liquidity management. The second is that COVID represents a decline in suppliers’ ability to punish defaulting customers. Next, we outline these two facets of our setting.2.1. COVID as a liquidity shockPrior work shows that liquidity shocks can propagate through supply chain networks through trade credit (Kiyotaki and Moore, 1997, Jacobson and von Schedvin, 2015). Specifically, when a customer faces distress they are typically faced with a decision of strategic liquidity management, which often translates to delayed payments to some or all of their suppliers. When faced with such decisions, customers take into account several factors, based on both “hard” data about a particular supplier and softer information like judgements and preferences. For instance, Costello (2020) shows that suppliers faced with a banking liquidity shock shrink the trade credit provided to some of their downstream customers but not to all of them. Thus, a large macroeconomic liquidity shock, combined with the discretionary judgement involved in making payment decisions, could give rise to discrimination – either implicit or explicit – playing a more substantive role in customer payment behavior.COVID indeed represents a substantial market-wide economic shock to liquidity. Seasonally adjusted initial unemployment claims were just over 200,000 per week through the first 11 weeks of 2020 and then jumped to over 3 million in the 12th week. By the end of April continuing unemployment claims surpassed 22 million. With respect to GDP, the consensus estimate for second quarter U.S. GDP provided by the Federal Reserve Board of Atlanta was an annualized decline of 35%. These economic effects of the pandemic on consumers led to a sharp fall in the demand for goods (Coibion et al., 2020). All of these factors likely gave rise to customers’ strategic liquidity choices we outline above. Though we use COVID as a setting, one can imagine alternative market-wide shocks to test our predictions, such as the Great Recession of 2008-2009.62.2. COVID and market-based punishments2.2.1. Discrimination in equilibriumIn the Becker (1957) model of taste-based discrimination, the discriminating employer has a disutility from hiring black workers, thus the marginal cost of black labor is higher than an otherwise equivalent white worker. A discriminating employer only hires white workers, and the market wage for black employees falls. This is referred to as the “wage gap” between the white and black employees. However several studies, including Becker (1957) and Arrow (1973), point out that discrimination’s impact on the wage gap should not hold in the long-run under conditions of perfect competition. This is because a less-prejudiced employer can earn higher profits than the discriminator who chooses to employ more expensive white employees, driving discriminators out of the market. In that case, market-based punishment should reduce the impact of discrimination on wages in the long-run. These studies argue that, to observe the effects of discrimination, there needs to be a market failure: something that reduces the market-based punishments observed in perfectly competitive markets.In line with these arguments, if one were to assume perfectly competitive markets then non-paying customers should face market-based punishments like rationed supply. In other words, in conditions of perfect competition discriminatory actions should be competed away making it difficult for researchers to capture.2.2.2. Discrimination with market frictionsAs highlighted above, capturing discrimination in equilibrium requires relaxing assumptions of perfectly competitive markets. We note that market frictions are common and thus discrimination may persist in certain sectors, geographic regions, or relationships that require specific investments. However, we argue that the COVID-19 pandemic presented unprecedented frictions to the supply chain; these substantial frictions may have allowed for a measurable increase in discriminatory behavior, relative to the pre-COVID period. Moreover, from an empirical perspective it may be easier to capture discrimination on-average during COVID, because the supply chain frictions and customer liquidity concerns were aligned for many sectors in event-time.We posit that the COVID-19 pandemic introduced at least two frictions to the supplier’s ability to use market-based forces to discipline non-paying customers. First, the sharp and unexpected onset of the pandemic left suppliers with imperfect information about customers’ ability to pay. The economic effects of the pandemic were widespread across industries. For example, while some industries had more severe effects (e.g., restaurants), all major sectors of the economy had substantial increases in unemployment rates by June of 2020. The pervasive adverse effects of the pandemic meant that suppliers expected across-the-board lapses in payment, with limited information about the payment behavior offered by outside customers. Imperfect information about outside options made it more difficult for a supplier to punish a discriminating customer.Second, the pandemic resulted in a sharp fall in the demand for goods and services. In the short-run, if demand fell faster than supply, existing customers likely had bargaining power over their suppliers. That is, a shortage of customers that continued to purchase through the pandemic means that suppliers had fewer outside options and a more limited ability to punish non-paying customers.2.3. Setting: The trade creditorWe explore the role of discrimination in the market for trade credit payments. Trade credit arises when a supplier provides temporary financing to a customer for a product or service exchanged in a business-to-business transaction. In a typical organization, a sales agreement is reached first, and then trade credit is allocated, monitored, and collected. Thus, the sales function within a firm is distinct from the credit function within a firm.7 First, a supplier and customer agree to transact during the sales decision — when a procurement officer at the customer firm is choosing among potential suppliers and when a member of the supplier’s sales team is identifying a set of customers to target. After a potential buyer–supplier “match” is made between the procurement officer and the salesperson, the transaction is passed to the individuals at each firm that are involved in trade credit. Trade credit payments are usually handled by the credit team on the supplier side and the accounts payable team on the customer side. In other words, individuals involved in the trade credit decision are rarely involved in selection of trading partners; rather, they “inherit” their trade credit relationships based on the match that was made during the sales process. Our setting explores the effect of discrimination in the decision of a customer to pay a trade credit invoice. Thus, the parties we focus on are (1) a member of the customer’s account payable team (the potential discriminator) and (2) an individual managing the credit team at the supplier firm (the potential discriminatee).
article.3. Data: 3.1. Sample constructionWe rely on a novel and proprietary dataset that contains granular data on accounts receivable for supplier-buyer pairs. These data are obtained from a third party credit aggregation and prediction platform (henceforth, the platform) that produces risk scores for millions of customers worldwide, based on trade payment data, credit bureau information, financial filings, industry trends, and other innovative data sources. Suppliers are required to produce a monthly report disclosing all transactions with customers; the report contains the current receivables and past due balances for their universe of buyers. In other words, all members must make a monthly upload of every transaction made with every customer.8 The data also includes receivables aging reports, which detail past due balances at the transaction-month-level, broken into 30 day buckets. The platform relies on this data to produce time-varying customer risk scores, which are shared with all member firms.We use the transaction-level data to build a panel of matched supplier-buyer pairs over the period October 2019 through May 2020. We apply a number of standard filters to the sample, removing suppliers flagged as factoring or other financial companies in the platform’s database. Our data span both public and privately held companies, represent thirty-two two-digit SIC industries, and range in size from small mom-and-pop stores to Fortune 500 companies. Anonymity is required by our data provider. Fig. 2 provides a snapshot of the data, where identifying information (including the last names of credit officers) have been omitted or anonymized. This data provides the basis for calculating late payments from customers to their suppliers; we calculate late payments based on two sub-samples. First, we use all supplier-customer pairs in the database and calculate the log of one plus the total amount of past due receivables (the last column in Fig. 2). We henceforth refer to this sample as the “full sample”. This full sample allows us to analyze the payment behavior for a large set of accounts, including transactions that were executed before the onset of the pandemic but that have gone unpaid. Take, for example, the final two rows in Fig. 2. Supplier S3 did not make any current sales to customer C1 during the pandemic, but customer C1 still has overdue bills to S3. Our full sample includes customers who do not continue to purchase throughout COVID, but who have outstanding bills.One concern with using the full sample of buyer-seller pairs is that it mixes the effects of non-payment with the effects of a demand-side shock. In our illustrative example, the decline in past-dues from C1 to S3 are due to both slower payment of existing bills and due to a decline in new credit sales (which may lapse to the past due bucket). Therefore, we create a second measure of past-due balances that relies only on the sample of supplier-customer pairs that continue to transact through COVID. For this subsample, our measure of past due accounts is the percentage of the most recent credit sale that becomes past due in the subsequent month, % Past 30 days. We henceforth refer to this sample as the “COVID subsample”.Download: Download high-res image (286KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Snapshot of anonymized data. This figure provides an example of the raw receivables data, where identifying information has been removed to preserve anonymity.3.2. Assignment to treatmentIncluded in the platform data are the identities of the individuals in each suppliers’ credit departments. The credit department is tasked with decisions related to trade credit. Specifically, they screen customers at the onset of the relationship to approve trade credit loans, monitor the customer throughout the relationship to make adjustments to the customer’s credit line, and they are responsible for collecting payment on each invoice. Though credit departments have a team of credit officers, the lead credit manager manages the workflow and is often the point person for personally following up with clients. Specifically, the lead credit officer will correspond with the customer on the phone or via email if a payment is overdue. Thus, the lead credit officer is the customer’s main point of contact when making payments on outstanding invoices. If discrimination plays a role in the customer’s payment decision, the supplier’s lead credit officer is likely to be a target. We first record the credit officers’ positions through the title that is listed in the database. Lead credit officers are identified through keyword searches of the title.9Our data report the first and last names of the suppliers’ lead credit officers, which we use to assign suppliers to the treatment group. We follow the methodology in Tzioumis (2018) to identify demographic aspects – specifically race and gender – of the credit officer’s name. To record gender, we use Genderize.io, which identifies gender based on first names using governmental sources and data from social media. To record the lead credit officer’s race, we use the ethnicolr package in Python which exploits U.S. census data, the Florida voting registration data, and Wikipedia data to predict race and ethnicity based on first and last name.10 The treatment group, Minority, is set equal to one if the lead credit officer is either female or Black.11Table 1. Descriptive statisticsThough our setting focuses on trade creditors, one may be interested in how our sample compares to other firms in the economy. Table 1 provides a comparison of our sample of suppliers relative to the Compustat universe. To do so, we first match suppliers in our sample to Compustat based on name and location; thus, Table 1 only reports descriptive statistics for the subset of publicly listed suppliers in our sample. On average, the public suppliers in our sample are similar, if not slightly larger (across all dimensions) than the average Compustat firm; however, the only statistically significant difference to note is in long-term debt, where our sample of public suppliers hold a larger balance of debt than the average firm in Compustat. We point out that the private suppliers in our sample are likely to be smaller on average than the public suppliers, which would bring our sample averages down.12
article.4. Empirical framework: We use a difference-in-differences (DD) setting to analyze how the pandemic changed payments to suppliers with minority credit officers, relative to suppliers with non-minority credit officers. To reiterate we do not suggest that discrimination in payments is nonexistent in the pre-COVID period, but rather that substantial changes in market conditions led to an increase in discrimination during the COVID period. Thus, the DD specification is appropriate to capture such changes. In the baseline specification, a supplier assigned to the treatment group if their lead credit officer is female or Black. The treatment period corresponds to the onset of COVID-19 stay-at-home restrictions in the United States: March of 2020.One key concern is that suppliers with minority lead credit officers may differ from the control group along some dimensions, which might be correlated with the outcome variables and would bias the DD estimation. We take multiple approaches to address this issue. First, we control for firms’ size and industry, as well as their interaction with the After indicator. Specifically, we split suppliers into quartiles of size based on the prior year’s (2019) aggregate volume of credit sales, and we include the size quartile interacted with After in our regressions. We also include the supplier’s industry category, based on two-digit SIC codes, interacted with After. These controls help address the concern that suppliers with minority credit officers respond differently to the pandemic than suppliers with non-minority credit officers.The DD approach compares the proportion of the supplier’s late trade credit accounts during the pandemic to the proportion of late accounts pre-pandemic, for both treatment and control firms. The DD is estimated by OLS using the following specification: (1)ɛyi,j,t=βMinorityi∗Aftert+γControlsi∗Aftert+αi,j+αt,j+ɛi,j,twhere y is the portion of overdue trade credit owed to supplier i from customer j in month t. After is a dummy variable equal to one from the beginning of pandemic through May 2020, and Minority is a dummy indicating whether the supplier’s lead trade credit officer is female or Black. As mentioned above, we include time-invariant control variables, interacted with the After indicator.13 We also include an exhaustive set of fixed effects to capture time invariant characteristics of the supplier and buyer as well as bilateral supplier-buyer relationships. The control variables, After indicator, and Minority indicator do not enter into the regression separately because they are absorbed by the fixed effects.We also follow Khwaja and Mian (2008) and include a specification with buyerXmonth fixed effects. This specification can be interpreted as the change in the timeliness of payments from a particular customer in a given month to a minority supplier, relative to the change in the timeliness of payments from the same customer in that month to other suppliers. Including these fixed effects requires that the downstream buyer has multiple suppliers, and it should absorb buyer-specific idiosyncratic shocks and the associated changes in the demand for goods and trade credit.
article.5. Baseline results: 5.1. Minority suppliers versus control firmsFig. 1 displays the time-series trend over the period October 2019 through May 2020. Suppliers with minority lead credit officers and suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers experience similar overdue payments in the period leading up to COVID, and pre-treatment trends appear to be parallel. The increase begins at the onset of stay-at-home orders but rises much more sharply for the treatment group in April of 2020. The slight decline in overdue amounts for both the treatment and control groups in May of 2020 is due to the coinciding decline in orders; in other words, total amounts due (current and past) declined because new orders slowed. We account for this decline in orders in our regression analyses.A key concern with studies on discrimination is that minorities differ from non-minorities along many economic dimensions that could explain different outcomes of interest. To provide further evidence on the characteristics of these two groups, we examine covariate balance in the pre-period across multiple observable dimensions. Table 2 reports the differences in the average pre-period characteristics of the treatment and control groups. We split the characteristics into three groups: supplier characteristics, customer characteristics, and relationship characteristics. Each of the characteristics are averaged at the supplier level. That is, customer characteristics represent the average characteristic for the customers in each supplier’s portfolio. Similarly, relationship characteristics represent the average characteristic of the relationships for each supplier. First, we assess balance on the basis of supplier characteristics. Eighteen percent of our sample of suppliers have a minority lead credit officer. Suppliers with minority lead credit officers are similar to suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers along most dimensions, with the exception of the average percentage past due accounts. Specifically, suppliers with minority lead credit officers have lower past due accounts than suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers. Though the two groups of suppliers are similar on most dimensions, we will nevertheless want to control for differences in supplier traits in our regression specifications.A second concern may be that suppliers with minority lead credit officers are matched with different types of customers (e.g., different industries, sizes, age, etc.), which may have responded differentially to the COVID shock. Thus, in Table 2 we assess differences in the traits of customers matched to the treatment suppliers versus customers matched to the control suppliers. We note that we are limited in the descriptive information that we have about customers in our sample. In particular, the “members” of the data platform that we rely on are supplier firms, who upload a feed from their accounts receivable system which typically includes a transaction number and a customer name, and in some cases, the name of the customer in charge of making payments. The data provider then supplements this with information about the customer’s location and other traits gathered in partnership with other data sources (e.g., Dun and Bradstreet). We obtain the minority status of the customer’s payment clerk for a limited number of suppliers that voluntarily provide the name of the individual that they transact with at the customer firm. We obtain the customers size and age from those customers that have been matched to Dun and Bradstreet data. Finally, we obtain the customer’s filing status through a fuzzy match to Compustat. Overall, there are no significant differences in customer traits across minority versus non-minority suppliers. Based on this set of observable traits, we fail to document any differences in these customer groups. Nevertheless, in our main regression analyses we will control for time-varying traits of the customer through a customer*month fixed effect, in an effort to more robustly control for customer-level matching concerns.Table 2. Multiple regression resultsTo test whether the demographics of the supplier’s lead credit officer impact the supplier’s ability to collect trade credit payments, we estimate Eq. (1) using ordinary least squares. The dependent variable is the suppliers’ overdue accounts receivable. The level of observation is a unique buyer–supplier-transaction. The primary coefficient of interest is the After∗Minority term, which captures the effect of the difference-in-differences. Standard errors are clustered at the supplier and customer level.As discussed in Section 3.1, we estimate our main regression on two subsamples, and correspondingly, we have two different dependent variables. The first sample takes advantage of all suppliers reporting to the platform (the full sample). For this sample, the dependent variable is the log of one plus the total past due amount on each account.14 The results from estimating Eq. (1) on the full sample are reported in Table 3, panel A. Note that a customer need not have a new credit sale to carry an overdue balance. Moving from column 1 to column 5, we incrementally add more control variables and fixed effects. For example, in column 2 we control for the time-varying effects of the suppliers industry, and in column 3 we include controls for the time-varying effects of size quartiles. In column 4, we include a seller-by-buyer fixed effect, to account for the fact that relationship-level characteristics may impact payment behavior. Finally, in column 5, we include a buyer-by-month fixed effect to absorb time-varying characteristics of the buyer (i.e., demand for goods). In all specifications, we find a positive and significant coefficient on the After∗Minority term, suggesting that suppliers with minority lead credit officers have a larger increase in overdue accounts after the onset of COVID-19, relative to suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers. The result is economically significant; the coefficient in column 5 of Table 3 suggests that minority suppliers have a 21 percent larger increase in overdue accounts, relative to the control group.In panel B of Table 3, we use the subsample of suppliers that continue to sell through COVID-19. We note that this is a much smaller subset of the data. For this subsample we are able to calculate the past due accounts as a percentage of the most recent credit sale, % Past 30 days. As in panel A, we incrementally add controls and fixed effects as we move from column 1 through 5. We find that, for those suppliers and customers that continue to transact through COVID, suppliers with minority lead credit officers have a larger increase in the portion of past due accounts after the onset of COVID-19, relative to suppliers with non-minority lead credit officers. The results are economically significant, suggesting that minority suppliers have a 9%–15% larger increase in the percentage of past due accounts, relative to the control group.5.2.1. Baseline results split by female and black credit officersThe results in Table 3 highlight that the demographic characteristics of the supplier’s lead credit officer influence their ability to collect overdue accounts during COVID. However, our treatment variable commingles the effects of gender and race. Therefore, we explore the demographic variable further by splitting our treatment variable into Female and Black. Table 4 reports the results. Similar to Table 3, we perform two analyses. Panel A uses the full sample and estimates the effects of demographics on the log of one plus the total past due accounts. We find a positive and significant coefficient on both the After∗Female term as well as the After∗Black term, suggesting that the effects documented in Table 3 are coming through both gender and race. However, across most specifications the magnitude of the coefficient on After∗Black is more than twice that of After∗Female. This suggests that suppliers with Black lead credit officers see past due accounts increasing by substantially more than other groups.Panel B of Table 4 uses the subsample of suppliers and customers that continue to transact throughout COVID. The dependent variable is the percentage of past due accounts from the most recent credit sale. While we find a consistently positive coefficient on After∗Female, the coefficient on After∗Black is not statistically significant in any specification. We note, however, that the subset of suppliers with Black lead credit officers who continue to transact through COVID is very small; thus, power is likely an issue in these analyses.
article.6. Within-relationship estimation: Our estimations thus far account for much of the variation driving differences in payment behavior to minority lead credit officers versus non-minority groups. Specifically, in our most rigorous specifications we absorb time-varying customer characteristics (like changes in demand), and we control for supplier-firm size and industry. However, the remaining concern is an omitted economic factor that is time-varying at the supplier level. One such possibility is that minorities face larger increases in constraints during COVID, leading to larger declines in their collection effort.While controlling for time-varying supplier characteristics is helpful in addressing some concerns, it does not fully rule out the possibility of an omitted, time-varying supplier characteristic that drives differential changes in collection. To account for time-varying supplier characteristics, we next move to a within-relationship estimation. Specifically, because suppliers have multiple customers in a given month and customers also have multiple suppliers in a given month, we simultaneously absorb time-varying supplier characteristics (using supplier*month fixed effects) and time-varying customer characteristics (customer*month fixed effects). Thus, we are able to examine how trade credit payments change during COVID within each supplier-customer relationship using the within-relationship estimator.In identifying discrimination at the relationship-level, our hypothesis is that the largest increases in payment delays should flow from customers with a greater inclination to discriminate toward suppliers with minority lead credit officers.We use location-based proxies to capture potential customer biases, consistent with Howell et al. (2022).15 In the platform’s database, we know the identity of the customer-firm and the location of the customer-firm. Thus, we match the zip code of the customer’s establishment to county-level or city-level proxies for bias.6.1. Empirical proxies for customers’ biasOur first proxy for discriminatory bias is based on county-level historical lynchings over the period 1877–1950. We obtain this data from the Equal Justice Initiative, who collect historical information on the number of lynchings of Black people in each county.16 Our proxy is the number of historical lynchings in the county, Lynchings. Our assumption is that customers located in counties with more historical lynchings are more likely to exhibit bias, particularly against Blacks. This assumption is guided by extant literature showing that historical lynchings can have lasting economic effects and can influence racial biases well after the lynching event (e.g., Cook (2014)).Our second proxy for discriminatory bias is based on city-level classifications of “Sundown Towns”. Sundown towns are cities or counties in the U.S. that have practiced some form of racial segregation through laws, ordinances, or violence. Towns are typically confirmed as Sundown towns in one of three ways: (1) Census figures reveal an absence of black people over multiple periods; (2) newspaper articles or historical records indicate laws or ordinances designed to keep black people out of the town; (3) oral history from experts indicates the town restricted people of color. James W. Loewen researched Sundown Towns and wrote the book: “Sundown Towns: A Hidden Dimension of American Racism”. We gathered information on Sundown Towns from https://sundown.tougaloo.edu/sundowntowns.php. Specifically, we assigned an indicator variable, Sundown, equal to one if the customer was located in a zip code classified as a Sundown town, zero otherwise.Our third proxy for discriminatory bias is based on individuals’ responses to the Moral Foundations Questionnaire (MFQ). The MFQ is a psychological questionnaire that includes Likert scale questions, aimed at eliciting an individual’s weight on universalist attitudes like “treating people equally” and “caring for the weak”. Enke (2020) aggregates the individuals’ survey responses to the county level to obtain a continuous measure of morality; more positive values represent counties with more universalist attitudes, whereas negative values represent counties with more “loyalist” attitudes (i.e., a focus on loyalty their own group, and a respect for tradition). He shows that universalist values are strongly predictive of economic behaviors such as the extent to which people donate money or volunteer time to charitable causes, and are strongly related to individuals’ voting patterns. We use the data from Enke (2020), provided on his website which indicates the county-level of Universalist attitudes. Our hypothesis is that individuals located in more universalist counties are less likely to discriminate against minority groups.Our fourth proxy for bias is based on Harvard’s Project Implicit Association Test.17 The implicit association test (IAT) was developed and validated to measure implicit biases by measuring the speed with which the respondent matches concepts to genders. Specifically, respondents are asked to sort male names and career-based words to one side of the screen and female names and family-based words to the other side of the screen. The reaction times for these tasks are used as a measure of the strength of an individual’s implicit associations. In other words, if one is faster at pairing male with career and female with family, relative to their pairing of male with family and female with career, that individual would be classified as having higher implicit gender-career bias. The gender-career IAT hosted on the Project Implicit site has been taken by over 954,000 individuals over the last 13 years, and responses are aggregated at the county level. Project Implicit aggregates individual test results to the county-level, which we obtain from their website. Our assumption is that customers located in counties with higher gender-career implicit biases (indicated by higher IAT scores) are more likely to discriminate against female lead credit officers.Our fifth and final proxy for bias is the customers’ state-level difference in men’s and women’s median earnings; specifically, the measure is calculated as the women’s earnings as a percentage of men’s (RelativeEarnings). We obtain information on the state-level median pay rates for men and women from the Bureau of Labor Statistics.18 Note that unlike our previous proxies, we do not have information on the pay gap at the county-level. We predict that customers located in states with higher relative female earnings hold less gender-based biases than those in states with lower relative female earnings.6.2. Aggregation of bias proxies: Principal component analysisTo construct a summary measure of customers’ discriminatory preferences, we aggregate the five empirical proxies using principal component analyses. Specifically, each of the five proxies is normalized, and we then conduct a principal component analysis to obtain a summary measure of customers’ biases. BiasPCA is the first principal component of the following bias proxies: Lynchings, Sundown, Universalist, IAT, and RelativeEarnings. The first principal component is the only component with an eigenvalue that is larger than one (1.288), and the weights on each component enter as we expect. Specifically, Lynchings, Sundown, and IAT enter with positive weights, consistent with greater values indicating higher biases. Universalist and RelativeEarnings enter with negative weights, consistent with greater values indicating lower biases.To better capture the type of customers’ biases, we split the PCA analysis into the constructs measuring discrimination on the basis of gender and, separately, discrimination on the basis of race. GenderBiasPCA is the first principal component of the following normalized bias proxies: IAT, RelativeEarnings, and Universalist. IAT and RelativeEarnings are more clearly targeted toward gender-based discrimination. However, we also include Universalist, since it is likely to capture the likelihood of general biases, including gender-based discrimination. The first principal component explains 37% of the variance in the three proxies and is the only component with an eigenvalue that is larger than one (1.119).Table 5. Within-relationship testsTo test whether minorities suffer greater increases in payment delays from their more biased customers, we supplement the DD specification with a triple-interaction term that captures the customer’s discriminatory biases. Specifically, we use the following specification: (2)ɛyi,j,t=βMinorityi∗Aftert∗BiasPCAj+γControlsi,j∗Aftert+αi,j+αi,t+αj,t+ɛi,j,t We now include supplier-month fixed effects in these specifications, which will absorb any time-varying changes in supplier characteristics at the onset of COVID, in addition to customer-month fixed effects, which absorb any time-varying changes in customer demand. We also include time-invariant relationship fixed effects (supplier-customer). Customer bias is captured by the three summary measures from our principal component analyses, discussed in Section 6.2.19Results are reported in Table 5. In panel A, we measure the customer’s county-level bias using the general bias proxy, BiasPCAj. The dependent variable in columns 1 and 2 is one plus the log of the total dollars past due between a buyer and seller each month, and the dependent variable in columns 3 and 4 is the percentage of the most recent purchase that is past due. The results show that biased customers significantly increase their delayed payments to suppliers with minority lead credit officers. Specifically, in column 1, the results can be interpreted as a one standard deviation increase in customers’ biases increases their delayed payments to minority suppliers by 10%; likewise, the results in column 3 can be interpreted as, for those relationships that continue to transact during COVID, a one standard deviation increase in customers’ biases increases their delayed payments to minority suppliers by 3.6%.We note that these specifications take advantage of the granularity of our data by including a rigorous set of fixed effects, which allows us to control for undesirable variation. Specifically, we include a time-invariant relationship fixed effect (supplier*customer), which will absorb the interactive effect of Minorityi∗BiasPCAj; we also include a time-varying customer fixed effect (customer*month), which will absorb the interactive effect of Aftert∗BiasPCAj, and a time-varying supplier fixed effect (supplier*month), which will absorb the interactive effect of Minorityi∗Aftert. Importantly, the only remaining source of variation in our regression model is time-varying payment behavior between a specific customer–supplier pair.We think that showing that payment delays increase at the onset of COVID for matched pairs of biased customers and minority suppliers provides a strong case for the role of discrimination in the payments chain. However, there could exist an omitted, bi-lateral relationship variable that also impacts changes in payments from “biased” customers to minority suppliers at the onset of COVID. For instance, a key concern is an omitted factor capturing the relative importance of a given supplier to a specific customer. Say, for example, that at the onset of COVID customers create a list of economically critical suppliers, and that the pecking order of payments follows that list. If all customers have similar lists, such that certain suppliers are always critical and others are always unimportant, this concern would be absorbed by the supplier*month fixed effect. However, if there is some variation in importance at the relationship level (certain suppliers are critical to some customers and unimportant to others) and these least important economic relationships are also those between biased customers and minority suppliers, then our inferences would be flawed.First, we assess the potential threat of a relationship-level omitted variable by creating four proxies for bi-lateral relationship importance. The first is the buyer-seller relationship “age”, with the idea that longer-duration relationships may be more economically important ones. The platform’s database includes a variable that identifies the first sale date, even if that date preceded the date that supplier joined the platform. We calculate the relationship age as the number of months between the first sale date and March 2020. Second, we calculate the volume of transactions between the buyer-seller pair in the year before the onset of COVID, as a proxy for how critical the supplier is to a particular customer. Sales Volume is calculated as the percentage of the supplier’s total annual sales made to each customer. Third, we calculate the distance between a buyer and seller pair. If customers prefer to buy local during the pandemic, we may see that they prioritize closer suppliers. Relatedly, we create a fourth variable to more precisely capture localization; that is, we define an indicator variable equal to one if the customer–supplier pair are both within 10 miles of each other. Note that all four of these variables vary at the supplier-customer (pair) level. As reported in Table 2, relationships between non-minority suppliers and their customers are similar to the relationships between minority suppliers and their customers along all dimensions except for relationship age, whereby minority suppliers have older (e.g., more important) relationships with customers than non-minority suppliers do.Though we find that bilateral relationships are largely similar across the treatment and control groups in the univariate, we still want to account for this threat in our regression framework. However, the potential omitted relationship-level “importance” variable lines up at exactly the level of variation we use to capture discrimination (Minority supplier * Biased customer * After); thus we cannot use fixed effects to address this time-varying economic importance concern. Instead, we interact the proxies for relationship importance, defined above, with the After indicator to account for the fact that relationship importance may differentially impact payments at the onset of COVID. For parsimony, we only tabulate the results of controlling for importance using sales volume, but results are similar when controlling for the other measures of relationship importance (tabulated in the Online Appendix). We split the pre-COVID relationship sales percentage into quartiles, where the lowest quartile represents the relationships with the lowest percentage of pre-COVID sales, and the highest quartile represents the relationships with the highest percentage of pre-COVID sales.In columns 2 and 4 of Table 5, we include the quartile of relationship importance, interacted with the After indicator. Consistent with importance playing a role in payments at the onset of COVID, we find that more important relationships have lower payment delays (relative to the least important relationships) during COVID. Interestingly, as we move from the second quartile of importance to the fourth quartile of importance, the absolute magnitude of the coefficients increases, consistent with economic predictions. Nonetheless, the coefficients on our main interaction terms, After∗Minorityi∗BiasPCAj remain statistically significant and economically similar to the coefficients in columns 1 and 3. These tests provide some comfort that variation in a relationship’s economic importance does not solely explain our results. That said, we urge readers to interpret our results with caution, given our proxies cannot perfectly capture all omitted time-varying, relationship-level concerns.To more clearly narrow in on the role of the customer’s specific type of bias, in panel B, we focus on the customer’s gender-based bias, GenderBiasPCAj and in panel C, we focus on the customer’s race-based bias, RaceBiasPCAj. We expect that gender-biased customers will delay payments more to females trade credit officers than to Black trade credit officers, and we expect that race-biased customers will delay payments more to Black officers than to females. Results reported in panel B indeed indicate that the largest increases in payment delays at the onset of COVID are from gender biased customers to suppliers with female lead credit officers. By contrast, gender biased customers do not appear to increase their payment delays to suppliers with Black lead credit officers; the coefficient on After∗Blacki∗GenderBiasPCAj is small in magnitude and statistically insignificant. Like panel A, controlling for relationship importance in columns 2 and 4 does not appear to explain our main result.Results reported in panel C show that the largest increases in payment delays at the onset of COVID are from race biased customers to suppliers with Black lead credit officers. Race-biased customers also appear to increase their payment delays to suppliers with female credit officers (in columns 3 and 4), which could be due to the fact that our measures of bias do not perfectly orthogonalize race- or gender-based preferences. However, it is notable that the magnitude of the coefficient on After∗Blacki∗RaceBiasPCAj is twice the size of the coefficient on After∗Femalei∗RaceBiasPCAj.
article.7. Punishment: A key argument in motivating the COVID-19 setting is that it presents substantial increases in market-based frictions, making it more difficult for suppliers to punish a defaulting customer. That is, when markets are relatively more competitive, a supplier with outside options may simply cut-off a customer or significantly ration supply to punish a customer that lapses on their trade credit payment. However, COVID made it relatively more difficult for suppliers to punish their customers, both because there was significantly more uncertainty in the marketplace and because it was more costly to find alternative customers willing to buy the supplier’s goods and services.To provide some evidence on punishment, we explore relationship termination after defaults (we label this customer “firing”). A relationship as terminated if the buyer-seller pair ceases to do business at some point in the 6 month period following a customer default. For each month, t, we define a customer as defaulting if any portion of the payment between the supplier-customer pair is late, zero otherwise. We then measure whether the customer–supplier pair ceases to transact during the t+6 month time-frame. Though we call this a customer “firing”, we should caveat that we cannot observe the reason behind the relationship break, nor can we observe which party initiated the break. For instance, relationships cease for many other reasons including shifts in supply/demand and other relationship- and market-specific conditions. Thus, we compare the average level of “firing” for defaulters, relative to a baseline level of relationship breakage for non-defaulting customers.We expand our sample to the earliest date that we have trading data (2014) through the last date that we have trading data (the end of 2020).20 Table 6 reports the results, split into the pre-COVID sample period and the post-COVID sample period. In the pre-COVID period, we do indeed find that suppliers (both minority and non-minority) are more likely to stop transacting with defaulting customers, relative to the baseline sample of relationships with non-defaulting customers; however, we do not find evidence of punishment during the COVID period; suppliers are equally likely to stop doing business with defaulting and non-defaulting customers.21 This pattern is consistent with the story that the COVID period relaxed market-based discipline for non-payment. However, we cannot say with certainty that suppliers did not punish in some other fashion (i.e., pricing), or whether they punished these non-paying customers at a later date after the COVID pandemic eased. article.8. Robustness analyses: We perform several additional tests to assess the robustness of our key results. First, one may be concerned that our results are an artifact of profit-maximizing customers paying the people that they have more information about. For instance, Fisman et al. (2017) find that similar religious and cultural beliefs increases lending from bankers to their like-minded borrowers in India. They find that their results are not due to discrimination (e.g., in-group preference), but rather due to mitigated information frictions in lending (e.g., a purely profit-maximizing story). Since our research question relates to discrimination, we wanted to rule out this profit-maximizing, information-based story. The information-based story would predict that customers pay the people that are in their same demographic group first. By contrast, theories of discrimination based on dominant-group preference predict that all people regardless of their racial or gender group discriminate against the minority group (see, e.g., Jost and Banaji (1994)). For instance, Ayres and Siegelman (1995) use a randomized trial to show that black and female customers fare worse (in terms of higher prices) when negotiating at car dealerships, even when the car salesperson themself is a minority; specifically, black customers fared equally worse whether they were matched with a black or a white salesperson.Thus, a way to disentangle the information-based rationale versus the discrimination story, we collect the race and gender of the customer in charge of making payments. The information on the identity of this customer is only available for a small subsample of firms in the data. We characterize customers according to their race and gender in the same way we classified lead trade credit officers. Specifically, we use Genderize.io to characterize this individual’s gender and the Ethnicolr package to assess race. Our results, tabulated in the online appendix, show that everyone, including female and black payment personnel, pay minority lead credit officers slower. The evidence is consistent with Ayres and Siegelman (1995), and is inconsistent with a profit-maximizing information-based story.The second robustness test we do is aimed at addressing the difference between statistical discrimination and preferences or biased beliefs. Statistical discrimination occurs when there is uncertainty about an agent’s type, and thus a decision maker draws inferences from a true, group-wide statistic. Schwab (1986) highlights that for statistical discrimination to contribute to decision-making, the stereotype must be true — rational agents make decisions supported by statistical fact. Bordalo et al. (2016) argue that this type of stereotype contains a “kernel of truth” because it is rooted in true differences between groups.To assess whether our results are due to true statistical differences between minority lead credit officers and non-minority lead credit officers (e.g., true differences in their ability to collect payments), we assess collection statistics in the period before the Pandemic. We show that for both high bias customers and low bias customers, their sample of minority suppliers exhibit faster collections than their sample non-minority suppliers in the pre-COVID period. Thus, it does not appear that true statistical differences in collections quality can explain our results.Third, though we use the Khwaja and Mian (2008) within-firm estimator, which requires the customer to be matched to multiple suppliers in the same month, one may still be concerned that certain types of customers are only matched to suppliers in the treatment group, and other types of customers are only matched to suppliers in the control group. Thus, while the customer*month fixed effects require each customer to transact with multiple suppliers in a given month, we further restrict the sample to ensure that each customer transacts with at least one minority supplier and at least one non-minority supplier in the same month. In this restricted sample, the within-customer estimator then allows us to compare how a given customer pays non-minority suppliers relative to how that same customer pays minority suppliers in the same month. Results from this most restrictive specification are tabulated in the Online Appendix and are nearly identical to those presented in the paper.Fourth, though our main analyses primarily rely on changes in payments at the intensive margin, we asses these changes at the extensive margin by creating a fully balanced panel of buyer–supplier pairs. Results, tabulated in the Online Appendix show that the largest effects flow through the intensive margin of payments, with smaller effects coming from the exit of certain buyer-seller pairs from the sample.Finally, we note that our primary identification concern comes from an omitted, time-varying relationship trait like economic “importance”. In the Online Appendix we supplement our control variables with additional proxies for relationship-level importance, including the number of years a particular buyer and seller have been transacting and the distance between the buyer and seller; results and inferences are unchanged. Nevertheless, we urge readers to interpret our paper with caution for at least two reasons. First, we recognize that our identification strategy, while compelling, does not fully rule out correlated omitted variables, particularly related to time-varying relationship level traits. Second, our setting exploits a narrow set of trade creditors and further places significant restrictions on the data through the complexity of our fixed effects. In doing so, we trade off the ability to more tightly identify potentially biased payment behavior, but we do so at a potential cost of limited generalizability.
article.9. Conclusion: We find evidence consistent with discrimination in the supply chain. In particular, we show that customers repay the trade credit to their suppliers with minority lead credit officers later than they pay their suppliers with credit officers that are white men. As with all studies on discrimination, a main concern is that minorities differ from non-minorities along important economic dimensions; thus, it is difficult to pinpoint the effects of discrimination per se. The novelty of our setting is the complex, interconnected web of supply chain relationships. Our within-relationship estimation allows us to simultaneously control for time-varying supplier characteristics and time-varying customer characteristics. The remaining variation – within the relationship – indicates that the greatest payment delays flow from biased customers toward minority suppliers.As an important caveat, bias is difficult to observe and measure. In fact, people are often unaware of biases that they may hold (Bertrand et al., 2005). Thus, we are forced to infer bias from economic choices. Our results in the paper indicate discrimination is at play; however despite our best efforts, other economic forces may explain more than what we are able to control for in our empirical tests.Our findings provide new insights into how discrimination manifests in economic effects for corporations. Specifically, by highlighting the role of discrimination in pre-established supplier relationships, we show that discrimination can play a long-lasting and economically significant role in firm level outcomes. We think that this has important implications for policy aimed at closing gaps between minorities and non-minorities. Addressing individuals’ biases and preferences (e.g., changing belief systems) may involve more substantial interventions than those addressing purely statistical beliefs.We also provide evidence on the types of discrimination that are prevalent in the United States; specifically, discrimination on gender and race both seem to explain customers’ payment behavior. Our findings are not only relevant to the academic literature on discrimination, but also to recent social movements highlighting the importance of minority group rights.
